title: The Presend State of the Jacquet-Rallis trace formula
speaker: Pierre-Henri Chaudouard
abstract: Abstract: The Jacquet-Rallis trace formula is a powerful tool to study periods of automorphic forms of unitary groups that show up in Ichino-Ikeda conjecture. In this talk, I will report on the present state of the Jacquet-Rallis trace formula. Then I will discuss the problem of the spectral expansion. (joint work with Michal Zydor).

proof_stages.label: Refined GGP period factorization and the local hypothesis
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the unitary-group setting for a quadratic extension, recalls the Ichino-Ikeda/refined Gan-Gross-Prasad period formula and its relative-character reformulation, and identifies the supercuspidal base-change hypothesis that the project aims to remove.

proof_stages.label: Unitary Jacquet-Rallis relative trace formula
proof_stages.summary: Jacquet and Rallis's first input is the truncated HH integral of the unitary automorphic kernel, for which Zydor's theorem gives geometric and spectral expansions whose regular terms are orbital integrals and whose cuspidal spectral terms are the desired relative characters.

proof_stages.label: Linear Jacquet-Rallis relative trace formula
proof_stages.summary: The second trace formula is built on the linear group ResE/F(GLnGLn+1) with Rankin-Selberg and Flicker-Rallis periods, producing a parallel geometric expansion and a cuspidal spectral factorization with the expected Rankin-Selberg and Asai L-values.

proof_stages.label: Geometric comparison and transfer
proof_stages.summary: Matching local regular orbital integrals imply equality of all geometric distributions on the unitary and linear sides, and transfer/fundamental-lemma results plus inner-form summation give a spectral comparison whose remaining difficulty is to isolate individual representations.

proof_stages.label: Regularized periods for the continuous spectrum
proof_stages.summary: To analyze the linear spectral side beyond cuspidal representations, regularized Flicker-Rallis and Rankin-Selberg periods are extended to moderate-growth automorphic forms using mixed truncation, Ichino-Yamana truncation, and analytic continuation of cone integrals.

proof_stages.label: Spectral distributions expressed by truncation operators
proof_stages.summary: A theorem of the speaker and Zydor expresses the spectral distribution attached to a cuspidal datum as a sum of integrals of truncated automorphic kernels over parabolic quotients, thereby linking Zydor's trace-formula distributions to the regularized periods.

proof_stages.label: Factorization for a generic cuspidal Levi datum
proof_stages.summary: For a Levi datum built from pairwise distinct cuspidal representations, the distribution becomes a sum over an orthonormal basis of products of regularized Rankin-Selberg and Flicker-Rallis periods and factorizes with the expected global L-coefficient, yielding weak nonvanishing consequences for associated unitary parameters.

proof_stages.label: Remaining analysis of general continuous-spectrum terms
proof_stages.summary: The program then turns to general cuspidal data, where one decomposes kernels by Langlands data, discards the purely discrete contribution, and studies induced cuspidal terms by exchanging adelic and complex integrations and tracking residual/Hilbert-integral contributions.

examples.name: Arthur trace formula as a contrast
examples.note: The usual Arthur trace formula, with weighted orbital integrals and non-invariance for the conjugation action, is used only to highlight why the Jacquet-Rallis relative trace formula is simpler in the generic-trivial-centralizer situation.
examples.engagement_reason: Arthur’s trace formula is invoked only as a contrast, with stated features like non-invariance and weighted orbital integrals but no computation or construction carried out on it.

passes.reason: Arthur’s trace formula is invoked only as a standard contrasting framework with non-invariant weighted orbital integrals, not as a structural classification exception or a counterexample.
the Arthur trace formula is a standard trace-formula object used only as a contrast with the invariant Jacquet–Rallis setting

examples.name: Splitting an improper integral at a cutoff
examples.note: The elementary picture of writing an integral as a sum over �- t and t-� pieces illustrates why an auxiliary truncation parameter can appear in the formula while the total expression remains independent of it.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly uses the cutoff split as a model, writing the integral as a piece from 0 to t plus a piece from t to infinity and reasoning that the total is independent of t.

passes.reason: Splitting an improper integral at an arbitrary cutoff is a routine analytic maneuver used as an analogy for cutoff-independence, with no special intrinsic structure or refutational role.
splitting an improper integral at an arbitrary cutoff is a generic analytic device illustrating cutoff-independence, not a structurally special or refuting example
